notes.note: P. Cornelius Lentulus Spinther, consul of 57 BC and Cicero's old benefactor in his recall from exile, had been captured at Corfinium and released by Caesar; he is now lying low at the seaside resort of Puteoli, uncertain whether to follow Pompey.

notes.anchor: the thirteenth day before the Kalends of April
notes.note: Romans counted dates inclusively backward from three fixed points each month --- the Kalends (1st), Nones, and Ides; this is 20 March 49 BC.

notes.anchor: came to me on the Quinquatria
notes.note: The Quinquatrus was the festival of Minerva, beginning on 19 March; Cicero often uses it as a casual date-marker, here fixing Matius's visit to mid-March 49 BC.

notes.anchor: nothing but proscriptions, nothing but Sullas
notes.note: Cicero fears that a Pompeian victory would bring a fresh round of proscriptions --- the published death-lists by which the dictator Sulla had liquidated his enemies in 82--81 BC --- with the Pompeian commanders cast as so many would-be Sullas.

"Arabia Felix" (literally "Fortunate Arabia") was the Roman name for the fertile, incense-bearing south-western corner of the Arabian peninsula; it figures here among the wild rumours of distant lands to which Pompey might withdraw and rebuild his strength.

notes.anchor: men whose creditors are reported to be holding meetings
notes.note: The Pompeian grandees named --- Scipio, Faustus Sulla, Libo --- were notoriously in debt; Cicero implies they need a victory and its plunder to satisfy their creditors, and so will stop at no crime to get it.
